answer:
**Slokāntara** (a Medieval Kawi prose work compiling and glossing aphorisms on *dharma*, vice, rebirth, and virtue) addresses the dangers of greed (*lobha*) explicitly as one of the six vices (*ṣaḍvarga*) that overrun the "field" of the human body (likened to untended farmland), leading to sin and degraded rebirth.[^1] 

The text warns: "*Lobha* (greed) means to desire the property of others and claim it as one's own. [...] The reason a person becomes sinful is because of the *ṣaḍripu*, the six enemies. Moreover, a pitiable person who is overcome by the six enemies will not fail to be terrified and to meet with sin." This occurs in the gloss on Ślo_41 (which contrasts worldly power from past merit against present heedlessness of *dharma*).

Greed also appears as **_ləja_** ("a greedy nature; great in craving, great in attachment and affection for evil") among the **Ten Stains** to avoid, as they are "not conducive to well-being."[^2] These stains parallel virtues but emphasize moral peril.

Coveting others' property is further damned as a hallmark of the degraded *cāṇḍāla* mind in the *Kali Yuga*, amid sins like theft and murder that lead to hell.[^3]

No single śloka is solely "on the dangers of greed," but these are the core warnings in its ethical schema of vices, rebirth, and *dharma*.

[^1]: *Slokantara*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-1d5638).
[^2]: *Slokantara*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-3a2b18).
[^3]: *Slokantara*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-898c82).
